title: Nobel Administration and Statutes
focus: Details the history of the Nobel Foundation, the addition of the Economics prize, and the statutes regarding prize division.
rationale: Fallback section derived from packet-level grouping.
sectionExcerpt:
[Page 072]
The Nobel Prize
• The Nobel Prize is among the most eminent awards in the world presented each year for intellectual achievement in six different fields of Physics, Chemistry, Physiology or Medicine, Literature, Economic Sciences and Peace.
• The winner of the Nobel Prize takes home a gold medal, diploma and 10 million Swedish Kronor.
• The prize money comes from the bequest of Alfred Nobel, the creator of the award.
• The sixth category, Economic Sciences, was not originally listed in Alfred Nobel’s will. It was established in 1968 by the Bank of Sweden in memory of Alfred Nobel and was first awarded in 1969.
• Post the death of Alfred Nobel, the Nobel Foundation was established to carry out the provisions of his will and to administer the funds.
• The Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences awards the prizes for Chemistry, Physics and Economic Sciences,
• the Karolinska Institute confers the prize for physiology or medicine, and the
• The Swedish Academy confers the prize for literature.
• the Nobel Peace Prize is conferred by the Norwegian Nobel Committee based in Oslo.

[Page 073]
* Since the start, in 1901, there are some years when the Nobel Prizes have not been awarded.
* The statutes of the Nobel Foundation says that a prize amount may be equally divided between two works, each of which is considered to merit a prize.
* If a work that is being rewarded has been produced by two or three persons, the prize shall be awarded to them jointly.
* In no case may a prize amount be divided between more than three persons.
